flashcards.back: An instruction code is a group of bits that tells the CPU what operation to perform. It consists of an opcode (operation code) that specifies the operation and possibly operands that specify the data to be operated upon.

flashcards.front: List the four phases of the instruction cycle.
flashcards.back: The four phases are: (1) Fetch - fetch instruction from memory, (2) Decode - decode the instruction, (3) Execute - execute the instruction, (4) Store - store the result back to memory.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Zero-address and One-address instruction format?
flashcards.back: Zero-address instructions use stack architecture - operands are on stack (push/pop). One-address instructions use accumulator - one operand is implicit (accumator), the other is specified in address field.

flashcards.front: Define opcode and operand in instruction format.
flashcards.back: Opcode (operation code) is the part of the instruction that specifies the operation to be performed (e.g., ADD, SUB, LOAD). Operand is the part that specifies the data or address of data on which the operation will be performed.

flashcards.front: What is a Three-address instruction format?
flashcards.back: Three-address format contains two operand addresses and one result address. Example: ADD R1, R2, R3 means R1 = R2 + R3. It provides flexibility but requires more bits.

flashcards.front: What is the function of the Control Unit (CU)?
flashcards.back: The Control Unit generates control signals that coordinate the activities of the CPU components. It controls the flow of data between the ALU, registers, and memory during instruction execution.

Microoperations are the elementary operations performed by the CPU during the execution of an instruction. They include operations like fetching, decoding, and executing, and are controlled by timing signals.
Addressing mode specifies how to locate the operand (the data) for an instruction. Common modes include: immediate, direct, indirect, register, indexed, and relative addressing.
The Instruction Register (IR) is a CPU register that holds the currently executing instruction. It stores the instruction fetched from memory until it is decoded and executed.
The Program Counter (PC) is a register that contains the address of the next instruction to be fetched from memory. After each fetch, PC is automatically incremented to point to the next instruction.
The Fetch Cycle retrieves an instruction from memory: (1) PC → MAR (address), (2) Memory read, (3) MBR → IR (instruction), (4) PC + 1 → PC. This brings the instruction into the CPU for execution.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Register Indirect and Direct addressing?
flashcards.back: In Direct addressing, the address field contains the actual memory address of the operand. In Register Indirect addressing, the address field contains a register that holds the memory address of the operand.
An accumulator is a register in the ALU that is used to store intermediate results of arithmetic and logical operations. In one-address format, one operand is implicitly the accumulator.

flashcards.front: What is the Decode phase in instruction cycle?
flashcards.back: During the Decode phase, the Control Unit interprets the opcode in the instruction register and determines what operation to perform. It also identifies addressing modes and generates appropriate control signals.
The Execute phase performs the actual operation specified by the opcode. This may involve ALU operations, data transfer between registers/memory, or control flow changes like jumps/branches.
